_meta.curationPolicy: Only skills/subagents/slash-commands from repos with explicit permissive licences (MIT, Apache-2.0, BSD, CC0, Unlicense). Originals attributed to author. Anything unclear or NC/ND → excluded.
_meta.notes: Single source of truth for the Claude Code Skills hub at /claude-code-skills. Every page reads from this file. Drift check in /pws-health should flag entries older than 90 days since lastChecked.

excluded.reason: No derivatives, no commercial reuse — safe to link, unsafe to republish entries.

categories.name: Git & version control
categories.description: Skills that handle commits, PRs, branching, and code history.

categories.name: QA, review, security
categories.description: Skills that review code, run tests, and catch bugs before they ship.

categories.name: Content production
categories.description: Skills that draft, edit, and repurpose written content.

categories.name: SEO, AEO, LLM-citability
categories.description: Skills that improve search visibility and LLM citation rates.

categories.name: Deploy & infrastructure
categories.description: Skills that handle builds, deploys, and runtime infra.

categories.name: Research & analysis
categories.description: Skills that gather, summarise, and synthesise information.

categories.name: Drift & regression monitoring
categories.description: Skills that catch decay in data, copy, and content over time.

categories.name: Meta: skills that build skills
categories.description: Skills that help you write, test, and maintain other skills.

categories.name: Legal & compliance
categories.description: Skills that check disclaimers, licences, and compliance gaps.

categories.name: Domain & portfolio ops
categories.description: Skills for managing multiple sites, vendors, or brand surfaces.

solid.label: Solid
solid.description: Useful in the right context, worth knowing.

situational.label: Situational
situational.description: Niche or task-specific — add when the need arises.

skills.name: Commit Helper
skills.purpose: Stages relevant changes, writes a conventional-commit message, and pushes — in one command.
skills.shortAnswer: The single most-used slash command for solo developers. Replaces the 'stage, message, push' three-step with a turn-by-turn workflow that writes a conventional-commit message you'd actually approve.
skills.whenToUse: Every time you finish a chunk of work. Best when your changes span 2-10 files and need a semantic commit message, not 'wip'.
setup: Save as ~/.claude/commands/commit.md with the instructions inline (git status, git diff, read recent commits for style, stage relevant files, commit, push).
Make sure your repo's commit-message convention is documented — the skill picks up context from .claude/rules/git-workflow.md or CLAUDE.md.
In Claude Code, type /commit when ready to ship.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /commit
Claude: [runs git status, git diff --staged, git log -3]
       Staged 4 files. Change: CTAs swapped to /claude-code-guide on 18 learning pages.
       Message: refactor: swap course CTAs → /claude-code-guide on 18 pages
       Committed → pushed to main.
skills.caveats: Don't combine with --no-verify. Skill should warn and refuse if hooks are skipped.

skills.name: PR Open
skills.purpose: Opens a pull request with a structured description drawn from the full commit range, not just the latest commit.
skills.shortAnswer: The PR description is the single most-overlooked piece of shipping work. This skill reads the full diff between your branch and main, groups changes by theme, and produces a summary + test plan that's actually reviewable.
skills.whenToUse: Any PR with more than 2 commits. Especially valuable when the branch has been open for a few days and you've forgotten what the early commits did.
setup: Install gh CLI and authenticate (gh auth login).
Save as ~/.claude/commands/pr.md. Skill should run: git status, git diff main...HEAD, git log main..HEAD --oneline, then draft title + body.
Uses heredoc to pass body so formatting survives.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /pr
Claude: [reads 11 commits + diff]
       Title: Claude-pivot content sweep
       Body: ## Summary (3 bullets) / ## Test plan (4 checkboxes)
       Opens PR → returns URL.
skills.caveats: For huge PRs (30+ commits) the body can get unwieldy — consider splitting the PR instead.

skills.name: PR Review
skills.purpose: Runs a multi-angle review on an open PR: correctness, style, tests, security, and docs.
skills.shortAnswer: Most code-review skills are single-pass. This one fans out into parallel reviewers — correctness checker, style nit-picker, test-gap finder, security auditor — and synthesises their notes into one comment you paste into GitHub.
skills.whenToUse: Before merging your own PR, or before requesting review from a human. Catches 80% of what a colleague would flag.
setup: Skill spawns 4 sub-agents in parallel, each with a narrow remit, then merges findings.
Needs gh CLI to pull the PR diff.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /review-pr 247
Claude: [4 agents run in parallel]
       Correctness: clean.
       Style: 2 magic numbers on L89-L91.
       Tests: missing happy-path test for the new branch.
       Security: no secrets; input validation on /api/upload looks thin.
skills.caveats: Not a substitute for domain expert review on novel code. Use for hygiene, not architecture.

skills.name: Security Audit
skills.purpose: Sweeps a repo for OWASP-top-10 issues, hardcoded secrets, insecure dependencies, and auth logic gaps.
skills.shortAnswer: A pragmatic security pass for indie projects that can't afford a real audit. Catches the stuff that actually shows up in breaches — leaked secrets, SQL injection, missing auth on admin routes.
skills.whenToUse: Before every public launch. Before adding third-party integrations. Quarterly on anything that handles user data.
setup: Save the skill in ~/.claude/agents/security-auditor.md (it runs as a subagent).
Configure scan paths in a .securityrc or inline — exclude tests/, docs/, migrations that intentionally show placeholder secrets.
Run /security-review or invoke directly from Claude Code.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /security-review
Claude: 3 findings.
       HIGH: .env.example contains a real-looking AWS key (leak risk if copied).
       MED: Contact form /api/contact lacks rate limiting.
       LOW: Next.js headers config missing strict-transport-security.
skills.caveats: Not a pen-test. Rule-based scanner with LLM judgement — can miss logic-level exploits.

skills.name: Test Writer
skills.purpose: Generates unit tests for a specific function or module, with sensible edge cases.
skills.shortAnswer: The test-writing skill that bridges 'I should add tests' and 'I have tests'. Takes a function, produces a test file with happy-path, sad-path, and 2-3 edge cases. Not a replacement for thinking about tests — a replacement for the boilerplate around them.
skills.whenToUse: On pure functions (parsers, validators, formatters). On business-logic modules with clear I/O. Avoid for integration-heavy or UI-driven code.
setup: Skill reads the project's testing conventions (.claude/rules/testing.md or looks for jest/vitest/mocha config).
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /test-writer lib/claude-data.js
Claude: Writing tests/claude-data.test.js with 8 test cases covering getModelById happy path, legacy lookup, null input, and tier filter.
skills.caveats: Verify the tests actually fail when the code is broken — skill can produce tautological tests if the implementation leaks into the assertion.

skills.name: Content Writer
skills.purpose: Drafts a blog post, newsletter, or landing page from a brief — using the site's established voice.
skills.shortAnswer: The subagent you reach for when you need a publishable draft, not a bulleted outline. Reads a content profile (voice, audience, do/don't), pulls research if a researcher agent ran first, and produces something you edit rather than rewrite.
skills.whenToUse: Daily, if you run a content site. Best when paired with a separate research pass so the skill isn't doing research and writing in the same turn.
setup: Feed it a .claude/content-profile.md per site — this is where voice, audience, monetisation live.
Invoke via /write <topic> from the site's repo.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /write 'Claude vs ChatGPT for content editors'
Claude: [reads profile, pulls research, drafts 1,800 words in the house voice, returns draft with suggested meta title + description]
skills.caveats: Writers quality depends on the content profile. Thin profile → thin output. Invest in the profile.

skills.name: Content Researcher
skills.purpose: Gathers sources, stats, frameworks, and competitive analysis for a topic — keeps research separate from writing.
skills.shortAnswer: The separation-of-concerns trick that levels up every content operation. Research and writing are different jobs; running them as one agent gets you mid-quality output. Run this first, then hand its output to a writer.
skills.whenToUse: Before every substantial content piece. Not needed for quick personal posts.
setup: Give it web fetch + search tools. No write access — research only.
Output: markdown brief with sources, stats, counter-arguments, suggested angle.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: Research 'Claude Code for writers'
Researcher: [fetches 12 sources, returns 600-word brief with 8 cited stats, 3 competitor angles, and 1 contrarian take]
skills.caveats: Web fetch can fail on paywalled sources. Configure fallback behaviour explicitly.

skills.name: Brand Voice Analyzer
skills.purpose: Analyses existing content to extract the brand's voice patterns, then scores new drafts against them.
skills.shortAnswer: Voice drift is the silent killer of content brands. This skill extracts your voice fingerprint from 5-10 existing posts, then catches when a new draft doesn't match.
skills.whenToUse: After hiring a ghostwriter. After bringing on an AI drafting workflow. Quarterly, to check your own voice hasn't drifted.
setup: Point it at 5-10 canonical examples in the site's /posts or /newsletter.
Produces a .claude/voice-fingerprint.json the writer skill can load.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: Score this draft against my voice.
Claude: Voice match 62/100. Drift: too corporate. Uses 'leverage' (you never do), passive voice 4x (you average 0.5x/post), hedges on claims (you're direct).
skills.caveats: Works best for writers with a strong, distinctive voice. Less useful for corporate or neutral styles.

skills.name: Copywriting Specialist
skills.purpose: Writes high-conversion short copy — headlines, CTAs, subject lines, hero copy — using classic copywriting frameworks (PAS, AIDA, 4Ps).
skills.shortAnswer: A different beast from the blog writer. Short copy is a discipline — tight, specific, benefit-first. This skill knows the frameworks and applies them instead of producing generic 'transform your business' filler.
skills.whenToUse: Landing page hero copy. Email subject lines. Ad creative. CTA button text.
setup: Frame each request with: audience, pain, offer, tone. Without that, output defaults to generic.
Ask for 5 variants — never ship the first.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: Hero copy for a Claude vs Cursor comparison page. Audience: devs deciding. Pain: wasting $20/mo on the wrong tool. Offer: opinionated side-by-side.
Claude: [5 PAS-framework variants]
skills.caveats: Frameworks are scaffolding, not the creative work. Treat output as a starting point.

skills.name: Headline Lab
skills.purpose: Generates 10 headline variants against different frameworks (curiosity, benefit, controversy, listicle, how-to) and ranks them.
skills.shortAnswer: Most content dies on the headline. This skill forces you to see 10 angles before you ship, then ranks them for predicted CTR and LLM-citability — because LLMs are the new SERP.
skills.whenToUse: Every post headline. Every newsletter subject line. Every YouTube title.
setup: Feed it: target keyword, audience, angle of the piece.
Returns 10 variants with a 1-line rationale each.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /headline-lab 'claude code skills' — audience solo devs.
Claude: 1. The 47 Claude Code skills that actually matter...
       2. We ranked 200+ Claude Code skills by licence. Only 47 are safe to copy.
       3. Your Claude Code setup is missing these 10 skills.
       [...10 total, scored]
skills.caveats: CTR predictions are directional, not data-backed. Don't ship without A/B testing for stakes-worthy pieces.

skills.name: Ghostwrite In Voice
skills.purpose: Drafts in a specific author's voice after reading 3-5 of their pieces — attribution-safe for transparency.
skills.shortAnswer: For agencies, ghostwriters, and internal comms teams. Reads sample pieces, extracts voice DNA, drafts new content that reads like the author wrote it. The ethical use is stated-up-front ghostwriting — not deception.
skills.whenToUse: Client ghostwriting. Executive comms. Translating between a brand's founder voice and a new team member's draft.
setup: Feed sample pieces. Skill produces a voice brief + draft.
Add disclosure language to the output — 'draft ghostwritten per client brief'.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /ghostwrite — attached: 3 Medium posts. Target: 1,200-word piece on vibe coding.
Claude: [analyses voice → writes draft that matches cadence, vocab, punctuation habits]
skills.caveats: Ethical concerns — use only where ghostwriting is known and consented. Never for impersonation.

skills.name: Newsletter Recycle
skills.purpose: Turns one blog post into a newsletter + 5 social posts (LinkedIn, Twitter/X thread, Instagram caption) in one pass.
skills.shortAnswer: Distribution math: every piece of content should ship 5-10 times across formats. This skill does the repurposing so you're not manually reformatting one piece for the rest of the week.
skills.whenToUse: Immediately after publishing a blog post. Schedule the derivatives for the following 7 days.
setup: Feed: URL or markdown of the source post.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /newsletter-recycle https://promptwritingstudio.com/claude-code-mcp-stack
Claude: ✉️ newsletter (400 words) + 🐦 thread (7 tweets) + 💼 LinkedIn post + 📸 IG caption + 📺 YouTube short script
skills.caveats: Output needs human polish — per-channel tone drifts if you ship raw. Budget 10 min per piece to edit.

skills.name: Twitter Algorithm Optimizer
skills.purpose: Rewrites a tweet or thread for the current Twitter/X algorithm preferences — engagement bait avoidance, read-time, link handling.
skills.shortAnswer: Twitter's algorithm rewards native content, moderate length, and replies. This skill rewrites your draft to play nicer with it — without descending into bait.
skills.whenToUse: When your baseline engagement feels low. When a thread underperforms what you expected.
setup: Keep this skill updated quarterly — platform rules shift.
Invoke with the draft tweet or thread.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: Rewrite this tweet for better reach.
Claude: 3 variants: (a) question hook, (b) pattern interrupt, (c) controversial take. Kept the link out of tweet 1.
skills.caveats: Algorithm advice decays fast. If the skill is older than 6 months, sanity-check against current platform guidance.

skills.name: SEO Audit
skills.purpose: Runs a full on-page SEO audit on a URL or page file — title, meta, H1, schema, internal links, content gaps.
skills.shortAnswer: A pragmatic SEO check that skips the 100-item generic checklists and focuses on what actually moves rankings: intent match, structured data, topical coverage, internal linking.
skills.whenToUse: Before publishing any page that targets a competitive query. Every 6 months on evergreen pages.
setup: Pair with a keyword research tool (DataForSEO, Ahrefs) if available — skill can consume that data.
Invoke with page URL or file path.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /seo-audit pages/claude-code-mcp-stack.js
Claude: 7 findings. H1 missing year (target query has '2026' intent). FAQ schema present ✓. Internal links: 2, target is 5-8. Related pages not cross-linked.
skills.caveats: No substitute for keyword research with real volume data. Pair with a paid tool or the /seo toolkit.

skills.name: AEO Citability Check
skills.purpose: Scores a page for LLM-citability — direct answers, quote-ready structure, source attribution, FAQ coverage.
skills.shortAnswer: LLM search is the new SERP. This skill checks whether your page answers the target query in a way an LLM would quote — clear claims, structured data, sources, no filler.
skills.whenToUse: Every new page targeting an informational query. Quarterly on evergreen pages as LLM search evolves.
setup: Give the skill the target query + the page.
Returns a 0-100 score with specific improvement suggestions.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /aeo-citability 'what is a claude code skill' pages/claude-code-skills/index.js
Claude: 68/100. Direct answer: strong (first 50 words). Structured data: FAQ only, add Article. Sources: 0 cited — add at least 3. No contrarian framing.
skills.caveats: Scoring is heuristic; calibrate against actual citation data from your /llm toolkit runs.

skills.name: Schema Generator
skills.purpose: Generates JSON-LD schema markup for a page — Article, FAQPage, HowTo, Product, SoftwareApplication, BreadcrumbList.
skills.shortAnswer: Schema is one of the highest-ROI SEO additions on modern sites — rich results, AEO boost, clearer LLM signals. This skill produces clean, spec-compliant JSON-LD from a page's content.
skills.whenToUse: On every new content page. Retrofitting schema on existing top-10-ranked pages.
setup: Feed it: page type + page content. Returns JSON-LD block ready to drop into <head>.
Validate with Google's Rich Results Test before shipping.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /schema-generator Article pages/claude-code-skills/commit-helper.js
Claude: [Article schema with author, datePublished, dateModified, headline, description, image]
skills.caveats: Don't over-schema. Google penalises irrelevant or spammy structured data.

skills.name: Internal Link Mapper
skills.purpose: Suggests contextual internal links for a page by scanning the rest of the site for topically-relevant anchors.
skills.shortAnswer: Internal linking is the lowest-effort SEO win most sites ignore. This skill scans your published posts for mentions of the current page's topic and suggests exact insertion points.
skills.whenToUse: After publishing a new page — add 3-5 inbound internal links. When ranking a specific page from position 20 → 10.
setup: Point at the site's /pages or /posts directory.
Invoke with the target page slug.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /internal-link-mapper /claude-code-skills
Claude: Found 12 candidate pages. Suggested 5 insertions — exact anchor text + sentence location.
skills.caveats: Don't auto-apply — some suggestions are contextually wrong. Review each.

skills.name: Meta Rewrite
skills.purpose: Rewrites meta title and description for a page, optimised for CTR on target query.
skills.shortAnswer: Meta titles and descriptions decay — what won in 2023 loses in 2026. This skill rewrites both against current SERP patterns and your target query's intent.
skills.whenToUse: Every 6 months on top-traffic pages. After a ranking drop. When CTR from GSC is <2% on a position-3-10 page.
setup: Skill needs: target keyword, page purpose, current meta.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /meta-rewrite for /claude-code-mcp-stack, target 'claude code mcp servers'.
Claude: 3 title variants (60 chars max), 3 description variants (155 chars max), each with a distinct angle.
skills.caveats: CTR requires real data to verify. Ship, measure in GSC, iterate.

skills.name: Deploy to Netlify
skills.purpose: Builds the site, validates output, and pushes to Netlify — with rollback if the build fails post-deploy.
skills.shortAnswer: The 'I just shipped' skill. Runs your build, checks the output dir exists, deploys, and waits for Netlify to confirm green. Rolls back on failure.
skills.whenToUse: Every production deploy. Replace manual netlify deploy commands.
setup: Requires Netlify CLI installed and authenticated.
Configure the site ID + build command in the skill or .claude/settings.json.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /deploy
Claude: [runs npm run build → deploys to Netlify → polls for 'ready' status → reports URL]
skills.caveats: Don't auto-deploy on every push — use Netlify's GitHub integration for that. Use this skill for on-demand deploys.

skills.name: Netlify Status
skills.purpose: Checks the latest deploy status for all (or one) Netlify site — green/building/failed at a glance.
skills.shortAnswer: If you run multiple sites, deploy status is the first thing you check each morning. This skill reports them in one table.
skills.whenToUse: Morning portfolio check. After merging a PR to confirm the deploy landed.
setup: Netlify CLI authed. Optionally feed a list of site IDs.
Invoke with no args for all sites, or /netlify-status <site-name>.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /netlify-status
Claude: 🟢 vendors.ie (2m ago) | 🟢 fluentatdesk.com (14h ago) | 🔴 readingcurator.com (1h ago — build failed)
skills.caveats: Only covers Netlify. Wrap for Vercel/Cloudflare Pages separately.

skills.name: Preview Server
skills.purpose: Spins up the project's dev server — handles npm install if needed, picks an available port, opens the browser.
skills.shortAnswer: The 'I want to see this locally' skill. Handles the tiny-but-annoying setup steps (port conflicts, missing deps, stale build) so you skip straight to the browser.
skills.whenToUse: Starting local dev. After pulling a branch with dependency changes.
setup: Skill reads package.json for the dev script.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /preview
Claude: Running npm install (stale node_modules)... Starting dev server on :3001 (3000 in use). Opening browser.
skills.caveats: For monorepos, extend to target specific workspaces.

skills.name: Kubernetes Architect
skills.purpose: Designs Helm charts, K8s manifests, and GitOps workflows — with sensible defaults for indie/small-team deploys.
skills.shortAnswer: Most K8s tutorials over-engineer for a scale you don't have. This skill gives you the minimum-viable cluster: one ingress, one service, sensible resource limits, no premature complexity.
skills.whenToUse: Only if you actually need K8s. 95% of indie projects don't — Netlify/Vercel/Fly is enough. Bring this in when you hit real multi-service orchestration.
setup: Feed it your app's services list + traffic profile.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /k8s-plan — 3 services, 50 RPS peak, single region.
Claude: [produces Helm chart + service manifests + ingress + HPA with 2-5 replica range, cost-optimised]
skills.caveats: Production K8s is still its own discipline. Use this to accelerate, not replace, expertise.

skills.name: Competitor Scan
skills.purpose: Scrapes 3-5 competitor pages on the same topic, compares stance, and identifies content gaps + differentiation angles.
skills.shortAnswer: The 'why publish this?' check. Before writing on a competitive topic, see what the top pages actually say — then find an angle they miss.
skills.whenToUse: Before any new page in a competitive niche. Monthly on your top-3 competitors' recent posts.
setup: Pair with a web fetch tool.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: Competitor scan on 'claude vs chatgpt' — top 3 results.
Claude: All 3 hedge on the verdict. None mention Claude Code. 2 have stale pricing. Gap: opinionated verdict + updated pricing + Claude Code angle.
skills.caveats: Scrape politely — respect robots.txt and rate-limit.

skills.name: Academic Brief
skills.purpose: Summarises 3-5 academic papers on a topic into a plain-English brief with the core claim, method, and limitations of each.
skills.shortAnswer: For non-academics writing on technical topics (ML, biotech, economics). Turns unreadable papers into defensible claims you can cite in your post.
skills.whenToUse: Tech/science content. Newsletter pieces that reference research. Avoiding hand-wavy 'studies show' claims.
setup: Tool access: web fetch (for open-access papers), PDF parse.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /academic-brief — 3 papers on prompt chain-of-thought. [URLs]
Claude: Paper 1: claim + method + limitation. Paper 2: same. Paper 3: contrarian — worth citing.
skills.caveats: Closed-access papers need manual download. LLM summarisation ≠ expert review — pair with real domain knowledge for stakes-worthy claims.

skills.name: Doc to Markdown
skills.purpose: Converts Word, PDF, or HTML documents into clean markdown — preserving structure, tables, and code blocks.
skills.shortAnswer: The 'someone sent me a Word doc' fix. Converts to markdown so you can edit in your normal tools, paste into Claude, or drop into a blog post.
skills.whenToUse: Client briefs in .docx. Research papers in PDF. Old content in HTML export.
setup: Depends on pandoc being installed (brew install pandoc).
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /doc-to-markdown brief.docx
Claude: [produces brief.md — headings preserved, 2 tables converted, 1 footnote inline]
skills.caveats: Complex layout (multi-column, floats) loses fidelity. Review tables specifically.

skills.name: Meeting Insights Analyzer
skills.purpose: Analyses a meeting transcript for key decisions, action items, sentiment shifts, and unanswered questions.
skills.shortAnswer: The post-meeting synthesis that nobody does well. Extracts: decisions made, actions assigned (with owner), questions left hanging, changes in tone that suggest unresolved friction.
skills.whenToUse: After any meeting where you have a transcript. Otter.ai, Fathom, Zoom AI Companion all produce one.
setup: Feed transcript text or file path.
Configure: attendees list, context (standup vs planning vs 1:1).
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /meeting-insights standup-2026-04-20.txt
Claude: 3 decisions, 5 action items (3 owners assigned / 2 unassigned), 1 unresolved — 'did we agree on the pricing tier?'
skills.caveats: Transcript quality drives output quality. Garbled auto-transcripts → garbled insights.

skills.name: Pivot Sweep
skills.purpose: Sweeps CTAs, URLs, or brand terms across a repo with tier-based policy — keep some, swap others, leave the rest.
skills.shortAnswer: When you pivot a product or brand, 20-50 files reference the old version. Manually hunting them is tedious and error-prone. This skill applies a tiered policy: Tier A keeps, Tier B swaps, Tier C needs judgement.
skills.whenToUse: After a brand change. After swapping an affiliate. After deprecating a product line and routing traffic elsewhere.
setup: Configure tier allowlist (filepaths that keep the old URL/term) and the new URL/term.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /pivot-sweep courses.becomeawritertoday.com/purchase /claude-code-guide
Claude: Found 33 files. Tier A (15 files): keep. Tier B (13 files): auto-swap. Tier C (5 files): need review — shown below. Confirm?

source.author: Bryan Collins (pattern from PWS Claude pivot, 2026-04-20)

skills.caveats: Don't run unattended — button text often needs rewriting alongside URL swap.

skills.name: Copy Align
skills.purpose: Greps for residual outdated copy across a repo after a pivot — catches prose that escaped the CTA sweep.
skills.shortAnswer: The under-appreciated follow-up to a pivot sweep. You replaced 'our course' CTAs but forgot about 10 FAQ answers that still say 'our course teaches...'. This finds those.
skills.whenToUse: Every time after /pivot-sweep completes. Before shipping a major rebrand.
setup: Configure a deny list of phrases that shouldn't appear on specific file groups.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /copy-align
Claude: 10 findings across 6 files. FAQ answers on education-ai.js still say 'our course teaches'. Fix?

source.author: Bryan Collins (pattern from PWS Claude pivot, 2026-04-20)

skills.caveats: Regex-based — prepare for false positives (legal text, schema.org fields).

skills.name: Drift Check
skills.purpose: Verifies a project's reference data (prices, models, deps) against upstream sources — flags staleness.
skills.shortAnswer: Reference data rots. Model pricing changes, products get deprecated, APIs move. This skill checks your data files against their upstream source and flags drift before it shows up in user-facing pages.
skills.whenToUse: Weekly as a scheduled run. Before shipping a page that depends on pricing or model data.
setup: Configure source pairs: local-file + upstream-URL + fields-to-check.
Can run as a GitHub Action on a cron schedule.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /drift-check data/claude-models.json
Claude: 2 findings. Opus 4.7 price matches upstream ✓. Haiku 3 retiresOn=2026-04-19 is past (today 2026-04-20) — move to status=retired.
skills.caveats: Upstream scraping breaks when sites restructure. Build fallback for HTML parse failures.

skills.name: Retire Model
skills.purpose: Automates the lifecycle: current → legacy → deprecated → retired as dates pass. Keeps model data in sync with vendor announcements.
skills.shortAnswer: Manual lifecycle management doesn't scale past 3-4 models. This skill checks today's date against each model's retiresOn, promotes statuses, and removes content-page references to models that are gone.
skills.whenToUse: Weekly. Automated as a scheduled run alongside /drift-check.
setup: Feed: data file with status + retiresOn fields.
Outputs a diff — you approve before apply.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /retire-model
Claude: 1 promotion: Haiku 3 → retired. Removes 3 references from FAQ on /ai-models. Apply?
skills.caveats: Don't auto-apply — a premature retirement flag (before vendor confirms) breaks pages.

skills.name: Site Health Check
skills.purpose: Runs a battery of drift/regression checks on a site — CTA hygiene, data freshness, link health, cruft in pages/.
skills.shortAnswer: The weekly audit every content site needs but nobody sets up. Checks whatever's important for that site's business logic — not a generic 'is it up' monitor.
skills.whenToUse: Weekly. After major refactors. Before quarterly business reviews.
setup: Custom per site — each site has different drift risks.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /pws-health
Claude: [runs 6 checks] 3 findings: PROSE regression on best-ai-tools.js, HUB-LINK missing between 3 Claude hubs, data freshness 3d old (green).
skills.caveats: Per-site bespoke — don't try to generalise into one skill. Every site has different drift.

skills.name: Hub Link Check
skills.purpose: Verifies that a defined hub of pages all link to each other — catches missing cross-links that break SEO and UX.
skills.shortAnswer: Content hubs only work if the pages in them cross-link. This skill defines the hub explicitly (a list of URLs) and fails if any page doesn't link to the others.
skills.whenToUse: After adding a new page to an existing hub. Weekly as part of site-health.
setup: Configure: list of URLs that form each hub.
Runs as a grep-based check across hub files.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /hub-link-check
Claude: Hub 'Claude hubs' (3 pages): claude-code-mcp-stack missing link to /calculators/claude-plan-picker; claude-pro-vs-max-vs-api missing link to /claude-code-mcp-stack. Fix?
skills.caveats: Grep-based, not semantic. A link in a footer counts even if it's not contextually useful.

skills.name: Skill Creator
skills.purpose: Scaffolds a new Claude Code skill with proper frontmatter, invocation block, and SKILL.md spec compliance.
skills.shortAnswer: The meta-skill. Writes other skills. Knows the SKILL.md frontmatter fields, the invocation patterns, and the file-location conventions so you don't have to look them up.
skills.whenToUse: Every time you're about to write a new skill. Replaces 'copy an existing one and edit'.
setup: Needs access to your skills directory.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /skill-creator pivot-sweep — refactor skill that sweeps CTAs with tier policy.
Claude: Created ~/.claude/commands/pivot-sweep.md with frontmatter, description, invocation pattern, 3-step instructions. Review and fill in details.
skills.caveats: Template gets stale as the SKILL.md spec evolves — review quarterly against Anthropic docs.

skills.name: SKILL.md Template
skills.purpose: The canonical SKILL.md template from Anthropic's official skills repo — frontmatter fields, trigger syntax, invocation patterns.
skills.shortAnswer: Anthropic's own reference. Start here before any community skill template — the spec changes, and Anthropic's repo is the source of truth.
skills.whenToUse: Before writing your first skill. When a community skill format doesn't work and you suspect it's out of date.
setup: Clone anthropics/skills and use their SKILL.md as your template.
Keep it in sync — subscribe to the repo's releases.
skills.exampleTranscript: [Not invocable — reference material, copy the template into your skill file.]

source.author: Anthropic
source.licence: Apache-2.0 (core skills only; docx/pdf/pptx/xlsx are source-available, exclude from forks)

skills.caveats: Check the repo's LICENSE and NOTICE files — Anthropic distinguishes between Apache-2.0 skills and source-available ones. Only fork the Apache ones.

skills.name: Skill Tester
skills.purpose: Dry-runs a skill against a test input to verify trigger detection, output shape, and side-effect safety before shipping.
skills.shortAnswer: The QA step for skill authors. Simulates invocation, checks the trigger matches, and verifies the skill doesn't accidentally write files or call APIs it shouldn't.
skills.whenToUse: Before adding a new skill to your .claude/ directory. Before sharing a skill publicly.
setup: Needs a test-cases file (JSON or YAML) describing inputs + expected behaviour.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /skill-tester pivot-sweep
Claude: 3 test cases. 2 pass ✓. 1 fails: tier-boundary case — treats a prompts/*.js file as Tier B instead of Tier A. Review regex.
skills.caveats: Dry-run simulates but doesn't guarantee — ship to a test environment before production.

skills.name: Legal Sweep
skills.purpose: Audits a site for a legal disclaimer page + footer link; creates and wires up if missing.
skills.shortAnswer: Every affiliate/content site needs a disclaimer, privacy policy, and (depending on geography) cookie notice. This skill audits the state and fills gaps — stack-aware, so it works on Next.js, Astro, and WordPress.
skills.whenToUse: On every new site before public launch. When adding affiliate links to an existing site. Annually for compliance drift.
setup: Configure: site template detection (Next.js pages router vs Astro vs WP).
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /legal-sweep
Claude: Disclaimer page ✓. Footer link ✗. Adding <Link href=/legal-disclaimer> next to privacy policy. Committed.
skills.caveats: Not legal advice. A disclaimer template ≠ bespoke legal review. Use for hygiene, not stakes-worthy compliance.

skills.name: Dependency Audit
skills.purpose: Checks every dependency's licence — flags GPL contamination, commercial-incompatible licences, missing attribution.
skills.shortAnswer: The dependency sweep most projects never do. Flags licences that force your whole project to go GPL, or require attribution you haven't given, or are outright banned in commercial use.
skills.whenToUse: Before every major release. When adding new dependencies. Quarterly on active projects.
setup: Uses npm ls / pip show / cargo tree to enumerate deps.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /dep-audit
Claude: 247 deps. 2 flagged: react-awesome-reveal (MIT ✓), lodash (MIT ✓)... one CC BY-NC-SA (usable but triggers attribution requirement — add to credits).
skills.caveats: Licence detection is heuristic — read the LICENSE file for anything novel or suspect.

skills.name: Vendor Add
skills.purpose: Adds a new vendor to a vendor-directory site — creates the JSON data file, the detail page, the category listing, and cross-links.
skills.shortAnswer: If you run a directory site, adding a new vendor involves 6-10 coordinated edits. This skill handles all of them from one description.
skills.whenToUse: Every new vendor. Bulk imports via a CSV-to-skill pipeline.
setup: Site-specific — configure schema + routing conventions in the skill.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /vendor Xero Ireland
Claude: [generates vendors/xero-ireland.json + pages/xero-ireland.astro + adds to category listing + cross-links 3 related vendors]
skills.caveats: Tightly coupled to site schema — clone-and-adapt rather than reuse as-is.

skills.name: Vendor Verify
skills.purpose: Verifies a vendor's data against their actual website — pricing, features, contact details.
skills.shortAnswer: Directory data rots faster than almost any content. This skill picks a vendor, fetches their site, compares against your stored data, and flags discrepancies.
skills.whenToUse: Quarterly on your top-50 vendors. Before publishing 'top-10' listicles.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /verify xero-ireland
Claude: 2 drifts. Stored starter price €12/mo; live shows €14/mo (vendor raised April 2026). Features list identical.
skills.caveats: Scraping can be flagged by anti-bot. Use a polite user agent + rate limit.

skills.name: Site Picker
skills.purpose: Presents a menu of your sites, loads per-site memory + recent git log, surfaces a session brief.
skills.shortAnswer: Running 10+ sites means every session starts with 'which one am I working on?'. This skill handles the orientation — picks a site, loads its context, tells you where you left off.
skills.whenToUse: Every session start when working across a portfolio. Skip for single-project work.
setup: Configure: list of sites with per-site memory files + paths.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /sites
Claude: [lists 20 sites] Pick a number.
You: 8
Claude: Prompt Writing Studio. Path: ~/src/promptwritingstudio. Last 5 commits: [...]. Open work: #38 CTA triage. What do you want to work on?
skills.caveats: Requires manual maintenance of the site list as your portfolio grows/shrinks.

skills.name: GitHub Actions Audit
skills.purpose: Audits GitHub Actions across all your repos for failures in the last 7 days — flags broken cron jobs, silent CI failures.
skills.shortAnswer: If you run 20 repos with scheduled Actions (scrapers, drift checks, builds), silent failures pile up. This skill asks gh api for the last 7 days and reports the red ones.
skills.whenToUse: Weekly. After a dependency update that might have broken CI.
setup: gh CLI authed against all target orgs.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /actions-audit
Claude: 3 red workflows. flashreport-scraper (3 consecutive failures, token expired?), bawt-deploy (1 failure, flaky). All green on 17 other repos.
skills.caveats: Doesn't fix — just surfaces. Triage red workflows manually.

skills.name: QA Swarm
skills.purpose: Launches 3-5 parallel QA subagents against a site, each with a distinct remit (SEO, monetisation, freshness, accuracy), synthesises findings.
skills.shortAnswer: Content QA is not one job — it's 5 jobs (accuracy, freshness, SEO, monetisation, link health). Running them serially is slow; running them in parallel is what makes Claude Code's agent model actually earn its keep.
skills.whenToUse: After a content batch lands. Monthly on evergreen pages. Before a major rebrand.
setup: Skill spawns N agents with narrow briefs. Collects outputs, synthesises into ranked findings.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /qa pages/claude-code-mcp-stack.js
Claude: 5 agents dispatched. Synthesis: 2 P1s (stale link, missing schema), 4 P2s. Full report below.
skills.caveats: Costs 5x tokens per run. Budget accordingly.

skills.name: AI Endpoint Smoke Test
skills.purpose: Fires a smoke test at AI-powered API endpoints (Claude, GPT, custom agents) on live sites — flags 5xx, timeouts, broken auth.
skills.shortAnswer: If your sites have AI features (Claude-powered tools, prompt optimisers), those endpoints break silently until a user complains. This skill tests them proactively.
skills.whenToUse: Daily or weekly scheduled run. After a provider pushes an SDK update.
setup: Configure: list of endpoints + expected response shape.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /ai-smoke-test
Claude: 3 endpoints tested. ✓ Vendors.ie /api/explain. ✓ FluentAtDesk /api/rewrite. 🔴 ReadingCurator /api/recommend — 500, Anthropic API key expired.
skills.caveats: Don't include sensitive test inputs in the skill — inject from a separate secrets file.

skills.name: Dead Link Crawler
skills.purpose: Crawls a live site for 4xx/5xx responses — reports broken internal links and rotted outbound links.
skills.shortAnswer: The classic site-hygiene check. Outbound links rot at ~10%/year. Internal links break when you rename routes. Run quarterly.
skills.whenToUse: Quarterly. After a URL structure change. Before a full-site audit.
setup: Uses a headless fetch + simple crawl.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /dead-links promptwritingstudio.com
Claude: Crawled 184 pages, 1,247 links. 3 broken. All 3 are outbound (rotted referenced articles). Fix list attached.
skills.caveats: Respect robots.txt on outbound crawls. Heavy on big sites — rate-limit.

skills.name: Core Web Vitals Check
skills.purpose: Runs Lighthouse against homepages (or specific pages) across a portfolio — reports LCP, CLS, INP, and regressions.
skills.shortAnswer: Core Web Vitals are a ranking factor. This skill runs Lighthouse across your portfolio in one pass and flags anything that's regressed from your baseline.
skills.whenToUse: Monthly. After major frontend changes. Before a redesign ships.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /vitals
Claude: 12 sites. Performance: 9 green, 2 orange, 1 red. Red: readingcurator.com LCP 4.2s (hero image not optimised). Fix list attached.
skills.caveats: Lighthouse scores vary between runs — trust trend, not single values.

skills.name: Licence Checker
skills.purpose: Verifies the licence of a GitHub repo (or specific file) before you fork, republish, or include content from it.
skills.shortAnswer: Before republishing anything from a public repo, run this skill. Fetches the repo's LICENSE, reports compatibility with MIT/Apache/commercial use. Saves you from accidental CC BY-NC-ND reuse.
skills.whenToUse: Every time you're about to curate, fork, or quote a public repo. Before building any catalogue page.
setup: Fetches the LICENSE file + README licence statement.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /licence-checker github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code
Claude: Licence: CC BY-NC-ND 4.0. Verdict: DO NOT REPUBLISH. Link-to only; no derivatives, no commercial reuse.
skills.caveats: Some repos bury licence in the README instead of a LICENSE file — skill needs to check both.

skills.name: Changelog Generator
skills.purpose: Generates a human-readable changelog between two git tags — grouped by feat/fix/refactor, with author attribution.
skills.shortAnswer: Changelogs are tedious but high-value. This skill reads your conventional-commit history between two tags and produces a clean CHANGELOG.md entry in Keep-a-Changelog format.
skills.whenToUse: Every release. Before drafting release notes for a public repo.
setup: Requires conventional-commit history to work well.
skills.exampleTranscript:
You: /changelog v1.2.0 HEAD
Claude: [produces Keep-a-Changelog section with Added / Changed / Fixed blocks, 23 commits grouped]
skills.caveats: Only as good as your commit messages. 'wip' commits break the grouping.
